content.subtitle: Main Modals
items: The nine main modals (will, would, can, could, may, might, shall, should, must) are invariable in form — they never change according to tense or subject — and they take a bare infinitive, which may be simple or continuous (eg could + do, be doing, have done, have been doing).
A bare infinitive in the passive is equally possible after a main modal (eg could + be done, have been done).

content.subtitle: Semi-modals
items: Semi-modals carry meanings that closely parallel those of modals. This group includes: need (to), ought to, had better and have (got) to.
While certain semi-modals, such as had better, remain fixed regardless of tense or person, others, such as have (got) to, do inflect accordingly.
Semi-modals can combine with main modals, giving rise to constructions like might have to.

rows: You **can't** genuinely **speak** eight languages fluently, **can** you?
There's no way you **could** decode that text when you were three!
I **can** pick up those notes later today if you want.
In the future, many people **will be able to** work from almost anywhere.
I **couldn't** perform in a talent show. I'd panic.
I **could** travel with them next month, but I'm not planning to.
They **could have** asked much tougher questions during the interview.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: The full negative form is usually two words, e.g. **could not**; the exception is **cannot** (one word).
**Can** and **could** do not work as infinitives; use **to be able to** instead.
✓ I'd love **to be able to** join you tomorrow, but I can't.
For a single completed event in the past, we normally use **was/were able to**, **managed to**, or **succeeded in**.
✓ Luckily, she **was able to** finish before the deadline.
With perception verbs (see, hear, feel, etc.), **could** is possible for one past occasion.
✓ I **could hear** that he was upset.

rows.example: **Could** I borrow your charger for a moment?
Yes, you **can** use my laptop for the presentation.
No, you **can't** park in this area after 6 p.m.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: In terms of formality, **may** is usually the most formal, then **could**, then **can**.
To talk about past permission, we commonly use **was/were allowed to**.
✓ We **were allowed to** leave ten minutes early.
In reported speech, **could** can refer to past permission.
✓ The teacher said we **could** submit the task the next day.

rows.modal: should, shouldn't, ought to, oughtn't to, had better
rows.example: You **should** back up your files before updating the system.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: **Hadn't better** is mainly used in question form.
✓ **Hadn't you better** check the venue details again?
**May/Might as well** can give advice when no better option is available.
✓ We **might as well** leave now to avoid traffic.

rows.modal: should have, shouldn't have, ought to have, oughtn't to have
rows.example: You **shouldn't have** replied to that message so aggressively.
You **could have** told me earlier that the plan had changed!
He **will** leave every light on in the house.

rows.use: Criticising one instance of usual behaviour
rows.example: You **would** forget your notes on the day of the meeting.
I **might as well** talk to the wall for all the attention I get.
You **have to** stay focused to finish this project on time.

rows.use: Lack of current or general obligation
rows.modal: don't have to, haven't got to, needn't, don't need (to)
rows.example: You **don't have to** attend every optional workshop.

rows.modal: will have to, must, mustn't, have (got) to, (will) need (to)
rows.example: We **will have to** submit the final draft by Friday.

rows.modal: don't/won't have to, haven't got to, needn't, don't/won't need (to)
rows.example: They **won't have to** retake the exam if they pass now.
I **had to** rewrite the report after the review meeting.

rows.modal: didn't have to, didn't need (to), needn't have
rows.example: We **didn't need to** print everything in colour.

sections.title: A: Circle the correct word or phrase
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: You **can't / couldn't** really ride a bicycle when you were eighteen months old, **can / could** you?
We **can / could** drop you at the station this evening, if it helps.
I wish I **can / could** speak Mandarin, then **I'd be able to / I could** read those signs.
items.answer: could, I'd be able to/I could

items.sentence: Excuse me, **could / may** you direct me to the nearest pharmacy?
I'm afraid we **can't / won't be able to** make it to the opening night after all.
Luckily, we **could / managed to** find a parking space just in time for the meeting.
Patrick couldn't **go / have gone** to the farewell dinner as he had to travel.
Would you like to **could / be able to** buy the house you're currently renting?
In five hundred years, **can humans / will humans be able to** live on another planet?
Dad, can I stay up to watch the match and do my reading tomorrow? No, you **may / might not!**
We could **catch / have caught** the earlier ferry after all, but we weren't aware of that at the time.
**Mayn't / Can't** I at least stay until the end of the concert?
Everyone **could / was able to** see that Marcus was uncomfortable with the question.
**May / Can I** turn the heating up a little? It's quite cold in here.
Because it was a family celebration, the children **could / were allowed to** stay up until ten o'clock.

sections.title: B: Verb gap fill
sections.instructions: Write a verb from the box in the correct form in each gap.

items.sentence: There seems to be nothing the authorities can ................. to prevent the situation getting worse.
He couldn't ................. anything in his right hand for several hours after the fall. He said it had gone completely numb.
I don't believe the director will ever manage ................. from such a public failure.
Can I ................. Sophie over for dinner on Friday? We want to try out a new recipe.
Tom has had another slice of cake so why can't I ................. one? It's not fair!
When I was young, my cousins and I weren't allowed ................. with the adults at formal dinners.
Hannah is intolerant to dairy, so she can't ................. cheese under any circumstances.
Chris had claimed he was fluent in Italian, but when we arrived it turned out he couldn't ................. a single word!
I'd love to be able ................. flexibly, but my current position simply doesn't permit it.
Rachel could ................. for the board of directors last year — she had the full support of her colleagues — but she chose to protect her personal life.
A travel cot for infants can ................. upon request at the front desk.
We couldn't ................. to the festival regardless, as Nathan had come down with a cold.

sections.title: C: Error correction
sections.instructions: If the word in bold is correct, put a tick. If it is incorrect, rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: Shouldn't you **getting** planning approval before you start the renovation?
Marcus really ought not to **be** worn smart clothes to the job interview last month.
She might have **gave** us a heads-up so we could have prepared.
You **should** better get a professional to look at that before it gets worse.
I don't think you **must** come in today if you're feeling under the weather.
We definitely **should** not draw conclusions before we have all the facts.
I probably won't get the scholarship but I **should** as well put in an application, I suppose.
You **could** have warned us you'd decided to cancel the trip.
We had **rather** reserve a table now, hadn't we?
Nina **would** forget to lock the front door every single morning.
He was going to be late, but then again he **will** be.
You **may** have let me know you were bringing a guest!

sections.title: D: Rewriting sentences
sections.instructions: Write a word or short phrase in each gap so that the second sentence has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

items.sentence:
I would recommend seeing a specialist if the pain continues.
You had ................. a specialist if the pain continues.

It was typical of Emma to forget to water the plants, wasn't it?
Emma ................. to water the plants, ................. she?

I'm annoyed that Oliver didn't mention the change of plans.
Oliver ................. the change of plans.

Choosing a bright orange jacket was a mistake – people keep stopping to admire it!
I ................. a bright orange jacket – people keep stopping to admire it!

Toddlers are messy, aren't they?
Toddlers ................. make such a mess, ................. they?

It won't do us any harm to browse the new menu before we decide.
We ................. the new menu before we decide.
items.answer: may as well browse/might as well browse

items.sentence:
Don't you think you should send that message by email rather than text?
................. better send that message by email rather than text?

It probably would have been sensible to ask your manager for guidance first.
You ................. to ................. your manager for guidance first.

sections.title: E: Word gap fill
sections.instructions: Write a word from the box in each gap. You will need two of the words twice.

items.sentence: I ................. have prepared so much food in advance; nobody touched it in the end.
Do you ................. to have the television on at full volume? I'm trying to read!
You ................. ring ahead before coming over, as we'll be home all day.
It wasn't easy, but I knew I ................. to make a formal complaint.
Kieran ................. need to apply for a grant as his company is already very profitable.
................. you have to attend the induction in person, or can you complete it online?
We didn't ................. to wear a uniform during the training week, so I dressed casually.
I think Elaine ................. to remain off work for at least another fortnight.
Passengers ................. not attempt to open the emergency exits during the flight.
We haven't ................. to vacate the premises for another three months, but we've started packing already.

sections.title: F: Documentary filmmaker (gap fill)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: As director of the most widely watched nature documentary series, I need (1) ...... ensure we deliver content that truly resonates with our audience.
Part of my responsibility is to highlight when a corporation has acted in ways it should not have (2) ......
or when an institution (3) ...... to have taken action but deliberately chose not to.
One criticism we face is that we think we (4) ...... not need to provide complete scientific evidence.
I have (5) ...... see verified data from a minimum of three independent sources before we include any claim.
As a production team, you have (6) ...... to get your facts absolutely right.
Looking back, there are moments when we (8) ...... not have been so cautious about naming certain organisations.
but we (9) ...... to have conclusive evidence before making any accusation.
Eventually, those being investigated realise they might as (10) ...... present their perspective on camera.
They understand they (11) ...... definitely have to speak publicly at some stage.
Businesses need to (12) ...... featured in the media regularly or they lose public awareness.
so they (13) ...... not complain when we come knocking, in my view.
you (14) ...... not fear our cameras if your practices are ethical and transparent.
you (15) ...... better brace yourselves, because we will find out the truth.

sections.title: G: Complete with must, have to, need or need to
sections.instructions: Complete using must, have to, need or need to in the correct form and the correct form of the words in brackets.

items.sentence: You ...... (pay) me back straightaway, though naturally you're welcome to if you'd like!
Helmets ...... (wear) at all times on the construction site.
If Sophie ...... (collect) from the airport tomorrow evening, maybe we could grab dinner in the city.
items.answer: needs to be collected / has to be collected

items.sentence: We ...... (depend) on traditional landlines in the future as wireless technology will be universal.
items.answer: won't need to depend / won't have to depend

items.sentence: The application forms ...... (submit) promptly, otherwise candidates will miss the deadline.
items.answer: need to be submitted / have to be submitted

items.sentence: Apologies for the delay. I ...... (collect) some papers from the office on my way over.
items.answer: had to collect / needed to collect

items.sentence: ...... (you / complain) in writing to get a refund, or was the staff member straightforwardly helpful?
Thankfully we ...... (take) everything Elena says at face value. She's misled us before.
items.answer: don't need to take / don't have to take

items.sentence: What time ...... (delivery drivers / start) their rounds every morning?
items.answer: do delivery drivers have to start / do delivery drivers need to start

items.sentence: I'd find it exhausting ...... (commute) for two hours each way every day, wouldn't you?
items.answer: to have to commute / to need to commute

items.sentence: Although we brought our own maps, we actually ...... (do) as the guide had everything we needed.
items.answer: didn't need to do / didn't have to do

sections.title: H: Circle the correct word or phrase
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: 'Did you hear a knock?' '**That'll / That must** probably be the delivery driver.'
'I had to cycle here in the rain.' 'You **must be / have been** soaking wet.'
'Your favourite band is playing tonight.' 'It **mustn't / can't** be. Their tour doesn't start until next month.'
'Clara made me redo the entire presentation from scratch.' 'You **must / can** have been furious.'
'Ben walked straight past our table without acknowledging us.' 'He **can't / couldn't** have noticed you.'
'I can't believe I got the last question wrong.' 'Don't be so hard on yourself. I **wouldn't / won't** have got it right either.'
'I'm hoping to get those limited-edition trainers tomorrow.' 'There was only one pair left, so they **may / might** well have sold out by now.'
'The sky has gone very dark.' 'Yes, I think it **must / might** easily hail this afternoon.'
'I hope she managed to catch her connection.' 'She should **land / have landed** by now, so I'd expect a message soon.'
'I hope there are still seats available.' 'We **should / might** just be in luck and get the last two together.'
Even in a thousand years, we ............... reverse the effects of climate change without radical action.
I ............... at the confidential notes on the manager's desk, but I decided against it.
............... Sofia better explain to her tutor what went wrong?
You ............... have at least informed us you were switching to a different supplier.
............... presentations at your college?
options: A Haven't students got to have delivered
D Don't students have to deliver

items.sentence: We ............... as well try to donate these old textbooks before recycling them.
The committee may well not ............... about the budget changes yet.
It is vital that all personal data ............... be encrypted before storage.
Fortunately I ............... a new travel adapter as I found mine in the side pocket of my bag.
I'm thinking of auditioning for the talent show; I'd love to ............... perform in front of a live audience one day!
